name: Egypt
tagline: Five millennia of civilisation along the Nile, from the first pharaohs to the modern Arab republic.
summary: Egypt is among the world's oldest continuous civilisations, born when the kingdoms of Upper and Lower Egypt were welded into a single state around 3100 BCE. For three thousand years pharaonic dynasties raised the pyramids, temples and a sophisticated bureaucracy along the Nile, before the land passed in turn to Persian, Macedonian, Roman and Byzantine rule. The Arab conquest of 639-642 made Egypt a centre of Islamic learning and power under the Fatimids, Ayyubids and Mamluks; Ottoman suzerainty after 1517 gave way to the reforming dynasty of Muhammad Ali and, from 1882, to British occupation. Formal independence in 1922 and the Free Officers' revolution of 1952 culminated in the proclamation of the Republic in 1953, since when Egypt has been a pivotal power in the Arab world.

founding.label: Unification of Upper and Lower Egypt
founding.detail: Egyptian tradition and the archaeological record place the founding of the Egyptian state in the union of the Two Lands—Upper and Lower Egypt—under a single ruler around 3100 BCE, identified with King Narmer (often equated with the legendary Menes) at the opening of the First Dynasty. The Narmer Palette depicts the king wearing both the White Crown of the south and the Red Crown of the north, symbolising a dominion that endured, in changing forms, for three millennia. The modern state took its present form on 18 June 1953, when the Free Officers who had seized power the previous year abolished the monarchy of the Muhammad Ali dynasty and declared the Republic of Egypt, with Muhammad Naguib as its first president.

quickFacts.label: Defining feature
quickFacts.value: The Nile, whose annual flood sustained Egyptian civilisation

quickFacts.label: World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Memphis and the Giza pyramid fields among Egypt's UNESCO sites

eras.title: Predynastic Egypt and the Unification
eras.standfirst: Farming communities along the Nile coalesced into rival kingdoms, then into the first unified state under a single king.
body: Long before the pharaohs, Neolithic communities settled the Nile valley and the surrounding deserts, developing agriculture, pottery and the Naqada cultures of Upper Egypt during the fourth millennium BCE. Out of these emerged two competing realms—Upper Egypt in the south and Lower Egypt in the Delta—each with its own regalia and gods.

Around 3100 BCE these were fused into a single kingdom. Egyptian tradition credited the union to a king called Menes, whom most Egyptologists identify with the Naqada III ruler Narmer. The celebrated Narmer Palette, discovered at Hierakonpolis, shows the king smiting his enemies while wearing both crowns, the earliest monumental statement of a unified Egyptian state.

The Early Dynastic Period that followed (First and Second Dynasties) established the institutions that would define pharaonic Egypt: divine kingship, a centralised administration, hieroglyphic writing and the royal capital at Memphis, strategically placed at the junction of Upper and Lower Egypt.

events.title: Naqada cultures flourish in Upper Egypt
events.summary: Predynastic settlements in Upper Egypt develop social hierarchy, craft specialisation and long-distance trade, laying the groundwork for state formation.

events.title: Unification under Narmer (Menes)
events.summary: Upper and Lower Egypt are united into a single kingdom under King Narmer, traditionally identified with Menes, inaugurating the First Dynasty and the dynastic state.

events.title: Memphis founded as royal capital
events.summary: A new capital is established at Memphis near the meeting point of the Two Lands, becoming the administrative heart of early pharaonic Egypt.

figures.name: Narmer
figures.role: First king of a unified Egypt
figures.blurb: Naqada III ruler of Upper Egypt commonly identified with the legendary Menes; the Narmer Palette commemorates his dominion over both Upper and Lower Egypt at the dawn of the First Dynasty.

pullquote.text: On the Narmer Palette the king wears the crowns of both the south and the north—the earliest image of the Two Lands made one.

eras.title: The Pharaonic Kingdoms
eras.standfirst: Across the Old, Middle and New Kingdoms, Egypt built the pyramids, an empire, and one of antiquity's most enduring cultures.
body: The Old Kingdom (c. 2686–c. 2160 BCE) was the age of the pyramid builders. Djoser's Step Pyramid at Saqqara, designed by Imhotep, gave way to the great pyramids of Khufu, Khafre and Menkaure at Giza, monuments to a powerful, god-king centred at Memphis. The collapse of central authority brought the First Intermediate Period.

Mentuhotep II reunified the land to begin the Middle Kingdom (c. 2055–c. 1650 BCE), an era of literary achievement and renewed prosperity, before rule by the Asiatic Hyksos in the Delta ushered in the Second Intermediate Period. Their expulsion launched the New Kingdom (c. 1550–c. 1070 BCE), Egypt's imperial zenith.

Under the New Kingdom, rulers such as Hatshepsut, Thutmose III, Akhenaten—who attempted a monotheistic revolution centred on the Aten—the boy-king Tutankhamun and the long-reigning Ramses II governed an empire reaching into Nubia and the Levant. Ramses II's clash with the Hittites at Kadesh produced one of history's first recorded peace treaties. After the New Kingdom, Egypt fragmented and fell successively under Libyan, Kushite, Assyrian and Persian domination during the Third Intermediate and Late Periods.

events.title: Djoser's Step Pyramid at Saqqara
events.summary: Imhotep designs the Step Pyramid for King Djoser, the first monumental stone structure in Egypt and a prototype for the pyramids to come.

events.title: Great Pyramid of Khufu at Giza
events.summary: King Khufu raises the Great Pyramid at Giza, the largest of the pyramids and the sole surviving wonder of the ancient world.

events.title: Mentuhotep II reunifies Egypt
events.summary: Mentuhotep II of Thebes defeats the rival Heracleopolitan kings and reunites Egypt, inaugurating the Middle Kingdom.

events.title: Battle of Kadesh
events.summary: Ramses II confronts the Hittites at Kadesh in Syria; the ensuing accord is among the earliest documented peace treaties.

events.title: Tutankhamun restores the old gods
events.summary: The young Tutankhamun reverses Akhenaten's Aten cult and restores traditional Egyptian religion; his intact tomb, found in 1922, made him world-famous.

figures.name: Hatshepsut
figures.role: Female pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty
figures.blurb: One of the few women to rule as pharaoh in her own right, she presided over a prosperous reign of building and trade, including the mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri.

figures.name: Ramses II
figures.blurb: Known as Ramses the Great, his long reign saw extensive monument-building, the temples of Abu Simbel and the campaign against the Hittites at Kadesh.

pullquote.text: The pyramids of Giza remain the only one of the seven wonders of the ancient world still standing.

eras.title: Greco-Roman and Byzantine Egypt
eras.standfirst: Conquered by Alexander, ruled by the Ptolemies and then Rome, Egypt became a Hellenistic metropolis and the breadbasket of empire.
body: In 332 BCE Alexander the Great took Egypt from the Persians, was welcomed as a liberator and founded Alexandria, which became one of the great cities of the ancient world. After his death his general Ptolemy founded a Macedonian dynasty that ruled for nearly three centuries, fostering Greek learning, the Library and Museum of Alexandria, and a fusion of Hellenistic and Egyptian culture.

The line ended with Cleopatra VII (r. 51–30 BCE), whose alliances with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony entangled Egypt in Rome's civil wars. After Antony and Cleopatra were defeated at the Battle of Actium, Octavian annexed Egypt in 30 BCE, making it a Roman province governed directly for the emperor and a vital source of grain.

Under Roman and then Byzantine rule, Egypt became a heartland of early Christianity. The Coptic Church traces its founding to St Mark, and the Egyptian deserts gave rise to Christian monasticism under figures such as St Antony. Doctrinal disputes—especially the Council of Chalcedon of 451—distanced the Coptic Church from Constantinople, on the eve of the Arab conquest.

events.title: Alexander the Great conquers Egypt
events.summary: Alexander expels Persian rule, is hailed as pharaoh and founds Alexandria, opening the Hellenistic age in Egypt.

events.title: Ptolemy I founds the Ptolemaic dynasty
events.summary: Alexander's general Ptolemy takes the royal title in Egypt, founding a Macedonian dynasty that rules from Alexandria for nearly three centuries.

events.title: Rome annexes Egypt
events.summary: After Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony are defeated at Actium, Octavian annexes Egypt as a Roman province, ending the Ptolemaic dynasty.

events.title: Council of Chalcedon and the Coptic schism
events.summary: Doctrinal disputes over the nature of Christ separate the Egyptian (Coptic) Church from the imperial church of Constantinople.

figures.name: Cleopatra VII
figures.role: Last pharaoh of the Ptolemaic dynasty
figures.blurb: The last independent ruler of ancient Egypt, she allied with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony; her death after the defeat at Actium ended three thousand years of pharaonic Egypt.

pullquote.text: Egypt became the granary of Rome, its harvests feeding the capital of an empire.

eras.title: Islamic Egypt: Caliphs, Fatimids and Mamluks
eras.standfirst: The Arab conquest made Egypt a Muslim land and, under the Fatimids, Ayyubids and Mamluks, a centre of power and learning with Cairo at its heart.
body: Between 639 and 642 an Arab army under Amr ibn al-As conquered Egypt from the Byzantines, founding the garrison town of Fustat near modern Cairo. Egypt was progressively Arabised and Islamised while retaining a substantial Coptic Christian population, and was governed in turn for the Rashidun, Umayyad and Abbasid caliphates.

In 969 the Shi'i Fatimid caliphate conquered Egypt and founded a new capital, al-Qahira (Cairo); the mosque-university of al-Azhar, begun in 970, became one of the foremost centres of Islamic scholarship. In 1171 Saladin (Salah al-Din) abolished the Fatimid caliphate and established the Sunni Ayyubid dynasty, leading the Muslim recovery of Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187.

From 1250 the Mamluks—a military caste of freed slave-soldiers—seized power in Egypt. They halted the Mongol advance at Ain Jalut in 1260 and presided over a brilliant era of architecture and commerce, with Cairo as the metropolis of the Islamic world, until their defeat by the Ottomans in 1517.

events.title: Arab conquest of Egypt
events.summary: Amr ibn al-As leads the Muslim conquest of Byzantine Egypt and founds Fustat, beginning the country's transformation into a Muslim land.

events.title: Fatimids conquer Egypt and found Cairo
events.summary: The Fatimid general Jawhar conquers Egypt and founds al-Qahira (Cairo); the al-Azhar mosque, begun in 970, becomes a great seat of learning.

events.title: Saladin founds the Ayyubid dynasty
events.summary: Saladin abolishes the Fatimid caliphate, restores Sunni rule in Egypt and establishes the Ayyubid dynasty, later retaking Jerusalem in 1187.

events.title: Mamluks seize power
events.summary: The slave-soldier Mamluks overthrow the last Ayyubid ruler and establish a sultanate that will govern Egypt and Syria for over two and a half centuries.

events.title: Battle of Ain Jalut
events.summary: Mamluk forces defeat the Mongols at Ain Jalut in Palestine, halting their westward advance and securing Egypt as the bastion of the Islamic world.

figures.name: Saladin (Salah al-Din)
figures.blurb: Kurdish general who ended Fatimid rule in Egypt, united Egypt and Syria, and recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187, becoming a celebrated figure in both Muslim and European tradition.

pullquote.text: Under the Fatimids and Mamluks, Cairo grew into the greatest city of the medieval Islamic world.

eras.title: Ottoman Rule, Muhammad Ali and British Occupation
eras.standfirst: An Ottoman province reshaped by Muhammad Ali's reforms, Egypt fell under British control before nationalist agitation won nominal independence.
body: In 1517 the Ottoman sultan Selim I defeated the Mamluks and annexed Egypt, which became a province of the empire while the Mamluk elite survived as a powerful local force. Napoleon's brief French occupation of 1798–1801 shattered the old order and opened the way for a new ruler.

Muhammad Ali, an Ottoman officer who took power in 1805, founded a dynasty that would rule until 1953. He built a modern army, industries and irrigation, and his successors undertook ambitious projects—above all the Suez Canal, opened in 1869. Mounting debt, however, drew in European creditors and undermined Egyptian sovereignty.

The nationalist Urabi revolt provoked a British invasion in 1882, after which Britain occupied Egypt while preserving the fiction of Ottoman suzerainty. Egypt was declared a British protectorate in 1914. The popular revolution of 1919, led by Saad Zaghloul and the Wafd, forced Britain to issue a unilateral declaration of Egyptian independence on 28 February 1922, creating the Kingdom of Egypt under Fuad I—though Britain retained control of defence and the Canal Zone.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Egypt
events.summary: Selim I defeats the Mamluks and incorporates Egypt into the Ottoman Empire as a province governed from Istanbul.

events.title: Muhammad Ali takes power
events.summary: Recognised as Ottoman governor, Muhammad Ali founds a reforming dynasty and modernises Egypt's army, economy and administration.

events.title: Opening of the Suez Canal
events.summary: The Suez Canal links the Mediterranean and Red seas, transforming global trade but deepening Egypt's debts to European powers.

events.title: British occupation begins
events.summary: Britain defeats the Urabi revolt and occupies Egypt, taking effective control of the country while leaving the khedive nominally in place.

events.title: Declaration of Egyptian independence
events.summary: After the 1919 revolution led by Saad Zaghloul and the Wafd, Britain unilaterally declares Egypt independent; Fuad I becomes king of the new Kingdom of Egypt.

figures.name: Muhammad Ali
figures.role: Ottoman governor and founder of the modern dynasty
figures.blurb: Often called the founder of modern Egypt, he created a centralised state, a conscript army and new industries, establishing a dynasty that ruled until the 1952 revolution.

figures.name: Saad Zaghloul
figures.blurb: Figurehead of the 1919 revolution and founder of the Wafd party, whose campaign for self-rule forced Britain's 1922 declaration of Egyptian independence.

pullquote.text: The 1919 revolution united Muslims and Copts, students and peasants, in a national demand for independence.
pullquote.attribution: On the rise of Egyptian nationalism

eras.title: The Modern Republic
eras.standfirst: From the Free Officers' revolution to Nasser, Sadat and beyond, Egypt became a republic and a leading power in the Arab world.
body: On 23 July 1952 the Free Officers, with Muhammad Naguib as figurehead and Gamal Abdel Nasser as the driving force, overthrew King Farouk. On 18 June 1953 they abolished the monarchy and proclaimed the Republic of Egypt, with Naguib as its first president. Nasser soon took control, championing Arab nationalism, land reform and non-alignment.

Nasser nationalised the Suez Canal in 1956, provoking an invasion by Britain, France and Israel that was reversed under international pressure—a political triumph for Egypt. He pursued state-led development, crowned by the Aswan High Dam, completed in 1970, though the 1967 war with Israel cost Egypt the Sinai. His successor Anwar Sadat launched the October War of 1973 and then, after the Camp David Accords of 1978, signed a peace treaty with Israel in 1979, for which he was assassinated in 1981.

Hosni Mubarak governed for nearly thirty years until the popular uprising of January 2011, part of the wider Arab Spring, forced his resignation. A turbulent transition saw the election of Mohamed Morsi of the Muslim Brotherhood in 2012, his removal by the military in 2013, and the election of Abdel Fattah el-Sisi in 2014, who has dominated Egyptian politics since.

events.title: Free Officers' revolution
events.summary: A coup by the Free Officers overthrows King Farouk, ending the Muhammad Ali dynasty's effective rule and beginning the republican era.

events.title: Republic of Egypt proclaimed
events.summary: The monarchy is formally abolished and Egypt is declared a republic, with Muhammad Naguib as its first president.

events.title: Suez Crisis
events.summary: Nasser nationalises the Suez Canal; an invasion by Britain, France and Israel is forced into withdrawal, confirming Egypt's sovereignty and Nasser's standing.

events.title: Egypt–Israel peace treaty
events.summary: Following the 1978 Camp David Accords, Sadat signs a peace treaty with Israel, the first between Israel and an Arab state, returning the Sinai to Egypt.

events.title: Egyptian revolution and Mubarak's fall
events.summary: Mass protests from 25 January force President Hosni Mubarak to resign on 11 February after nearly thirty years in power.

figures.name: Gamal Abdel Nasser
figures.blurb: Leader of the 1952 revolution and architect of Arab nationalism, he nationalised the Suez Canal, championed non-alignment and pursued state-led modernisation including the Aswan High Dam.

figures.name: Anwar Sadat
figures.blurb: He launched the October War of 1973 and then made peace with Israel through the Camp David Accords and the 1979 treaty, winning the Nobel Peace Prize before his assassination in 1981.

pullquote.text: With the proclamation of the Republic in 1953, more than two thousand years of monarchical rule in Egypt came to an end.
pullquote.attribution: On the founding of the republic

figures.name: Gamal Abdel Nasser
figures.role: President of Egypt and Arab nationalist leader
figures.blurb: The dominant figure of the republican era, he led Egypt from the 1952 revolution, nationalised the Suez Canal and made the country a leader of the non-aligned Arab world.

figures.name: Cleopatra VII
figures.blurb: The final ruler of the Ptolemaic dynasty, whose death marked the end of three thousand years of independent pharaonic Egypt and its absorption into Rome.

sources.label: Ancient Egypt: The Old Kingdom and the First Intermediate Period
Memphis and its Necropolis – the Pyramid Fields from Giza to Dahshur
